channel.title: CTV News – Queen's Park
channel.description: Queen's Park coverage from CTV News Toronto.

item.title: Ontario failed to collect nearly $10M owed by alleged animal abusers: documents
Ontario pulls funding for last provincially supported supervised consumption site
item.description: Ontario is pulling funding for the last remaining provincially funded supervised drug consumption site, after announcing funding cuts at seven others last month.

item.title: Ontario education minister says ‘significant’ legislation on school board changes coming Monday
item.description: Ontario Education Minister Paul Calandra says the government will be introducing “significant” legislation on school board governance on April 13.

item.title: Northlander train begins final testing before service resumes this year
item.description: The return of passenger rail service connecting northern Ontario to the south took another step forward Thursday as the Northlander began full testing of the routes and all systems needed before the trains are relaunched.

item.title: Ontario electricity system to add 14 new solar, wind projects
item.description: Ontario’s electricity system operator is giving long-term energy generation contracts to 12 new solar and two wind projects.

item.title: GTHA transit unions call on Ford government to halt expansion of fare integration program
item.description: Local transit unions in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area are calling on the Doug Ford government not to push forward with its plan to harmonize fares across the region, saying they have serious concerns about the move.

item.title: Ontario to extend WSIB coverage in privately run retirement and group homes
item.description: Ontario is planning to extend workplace safety and insurance coverage to health-care and support workers in privately run retirement homes and group homes.

item.title: Environmental groups launch constitutional challenge over Ontario's special economic zones
item.description: Several environmental groups have launched a constitutional challenge seeking to kill an Ontario law that allows cabinet to suspend other laws, arguing the Doug Ford government has abdicated the role of the legislature.

item.title: Could this be the end of green building standards in Ontario — again?
item.description: Premier Doug Ford’s government is taking another swipe at green standards while nixing a requirement for municipalities to build climate change goals into their official plans.

item.title: Loss of language and reading programs at TCDSB ‘devastating,’ parents say
item.description: Parents of students who take part in an international language program at the Toronto Catholic District School Board (TCDSB) are speaking out after the sudden announcement that the program would be axed as of next year for budget reasons.

item.title: Ontario shuts down one way of funding nurse practitioners as province misses deadline
item.description: Ontario’s health minister is shutting down one avenue of publicly funding all nurse practitioners, as the province misses a federal deadline for ensuring their medically necessary services are covered.

item.title: Ontario minimum wage workers to get raise on Oct. 1. Here’s how much they’ll make
item.description: Ontario’s minimum wage is set to rise to $17.95 an hour on Oct. 1.

item.title: Premier Doug Ford heads to Houston for Texas trade mission
item.description: Premier Doug Ford is in Texas this week on a trade mission where he will meet with companies and legislators to 'advocate for tariff-free trade' between Canada and the United States, his office says.

item.title: Ontario is cracking down on fare evasion on GO Transit. Here are the new steeper fines being proposed
item.description: The Ontario government says it is eyeing a raft of transit changes that could include harmonized fare prices across the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area and improved services across municipal boundaries.

item.title: ‘Historic agreement:’ Feds, Ontario invest $8.8 B to cut development charges in half
item.description: The federal and provincial governments will jointly spend $8.8 billion to lower municipal development charges that drive up housing costs over the next decade, Prime Minister Mark Carney and Premier Doug Ford announced Monday.
